name: SWE-EVO — Benchmarking Coding Agents in Long-Horizon Software Evolution Scenarios
year_developed: 2025 (initial arXiv submission December 20, 2025; updated through v5, April 4, 2026)
progenitor: Minh V. T. Thai, Tue Le, Dung Nguyen Manh, Huy Phan Nhat, Nghi D. Q. Bui (FPT Software AI Center / University of Melbourne)
driving_insight: Existing coding benchmarks (SWE-Bench) evaluate agents on isolated, single-issue tasks — fix a bug, add a small feature, typically touching 2 files and 33 lines. Real software development requires interpreting high-level requirements, coordinating changes across many files, and evolving codebases over multiple iterations while preserving backward compatibility. SWE-EVO constructs 48 tasks from release notes of 7 mature Python projects, each spanning an average of 21 files with 610 lines changed and validated by 874 tests. The benchmark reveals a 'striking capability gap': GPT-5 achieves 65-73% on SWE-Bench Verified but only 21-25% on SWE-EVO, proving that multi-step decomposition and long-horizon planning — not single-task execution — remains the critical unsolved frontier for AI coding agents.
decision_style: The benchmark itself is a measurement instrument, not a decision-making system. It evaluates agent decision-making through two lenses: (1) Pass Rate — binary success/failure on task completion, and (2) Fix Rate — a novel metric capturing partial progress where 60%+ of failures stem from requirement understanding errors. This dual-metric design reveals that current agents make poor decisions about task decomposition, scope interpretation, and multi-file coordination even when they can write syntactically correct code. The decision style being measured is LLM-driven deliberation — agents must decide what to change, how to coordinate changes, and how to verify correctness, and they fail predominantly at the decomposition stage.
problem_class_targeted: Long-horizon, multi-file, multi-step software evolution tasks in mature codebases. These are ill-defined problems with ambiguous requirements expressed in high-level natural language (average 2,390-word descriptions), requiring coordination across 21+ files, where any change that breaks existing tests is a failure. The problem class is dynamic (codebase state evolves), large (874 tests for validation), and partially decomposable (changes have dependencies but agents must discover them). This stands in stark contrast to SWE-Bench's well-defined, single-issue, 2-file, 33-line bug fixes.
ordered_phases: SWE-EVO is a benchmark, not a workflow, and does not prescribe phases. However, it implicitly tests agents across the full software evolution lifecycle: (1) Requirement Interpretation — parse high-level release-note-style descriptions into actionable change specifications; (2) Multi-File Coordination — identify which of the 21+ files need changes and the dependency order; (3) Iterative Implementation — make changes across files, respecting their inter-dependencies; (4) Validation — pass the project's existing 874-test suite and new task-specific tests; (5) Backward Compatibility — ensure no existing functionality is broken (PASS_TO_PASS constraint). The benchmark is structured so that skipping any phase results in failure.
feedback_loops: Implicit in the agent execution pipeline: agents can (and must) run tests after changes, observe failures, and iterate. The Fix Rate metric explicitly captures this loop — agents that make partial progress but cannot fully converge are credited for their progress rather than receiving zero. The feedback loop is the standard agent-environment interaction: agent makes changes, runs test suite, observes pass/fail, adjusts. However, the benchmark does not prescribe or constrain this loop — agents are free to implement their own feedback mechanisms.
gates: SWE-EVO imposes hard validation gates via test suites: (1) PASS_TO_PASS constraint — any change that breaks a previously passing test counts as failure, regardless of new functionality added; (2) the task's test suite must pass in full for the binary Pass Rate metric; (3) Fix Rate softens this gate by allowing partial credit for agent-intended tests. There are no human approval gates — this is a fully automated evaluation benchmark. The PASS_TO_PASS constraint is the most stringent gate and accounts for many failures where agents introduce regressions in files they did not intend to modify.
exit_criteria: For each task: all task-specific tests pass AND all pre-existing tests continue to pass (PASS_TO_PASS). Fix Rate provides a partial-completion state: tasks where the agent made changes that pass some but not all tests. The benchmark's overall evaluation is complete when all 48 tasks have been evaluated against these criteria. There is no concept of 'partial credit' for the Pass Rate metric — it is binary.
decomposition_method: SWE-EVO does not prescribe a decomposition method — it measures whether agents can decompose effectively. The benchmark's design reveals that requirement-level decomposition is the primary failure mode: 60%+ of GPT-5's errors come from misunderstanding what needs to be done, not from inability to implement. This is dependency-driven decomposition with no guidance — agents receive a high-level description and must self-discover: which of 21+ files need changes, how those changes depend on each other, and in what order to make them. The benchmark's 48 tasks implicitly test whether the agent's decomposition strategy is correct. The average task complexity (21 files, 610 lines, 874 tests) means naive flat decomposition fails — hierarchical, dependency-aware decomposition is required.
granularity_guidance: Not prescribed by the benchmark. Agents must determine their own granularity. The average task requires changes across 21 files with 610 lines, suggesting an effective decomposition would identify 20-40 atomic change units, each touching a few files with clear dependencies. The actual granularity used by evaluated agents is not reported in the paper summary. The Fix Rate metric provides indirect guidance: tasks where agents achieve high Fix Rate but low Pass Rate suggest decomposition was approximately correct but execution or integration was flawed.
boundary_rules: Enforced via PASS_TO_PASS constraint: any change that bleeds into a file not intended for modification and breaks existing tests causes failure. This is a hard boundary rule: agents must modify only what the task requires and nothing else. The 21-file average tests whether agents can respect boundaries across a large file surface. Unlike MECE (human-defined boundaries), SWE-EVO's boundaries are emergent — the test suite defines correctness boundaries, and agents must discover them through iteration. Failure to respect boundaries (unintended modifications, regressions) is penalized severely.
risk_assessment_mechanism: SWE-EVO does not include a risk assessment mechanism — it is a benchmark, not an operational system. Risk is implicitly evaluated through the PASS_TO_PASS constraint: any change carries the risk of breaking existing functionality, and the 874-test suite detects this. The benchmark's difficulty (most models at 10% or below) effectively assesses risk exposure: the gap between SWE-Bench performance and SWE-EVO performance quantifies the risk of applying single-task agents to multi-file evolution scenarios. For agents evaluated on the benchmark, risk assessment is whatever the agent framework provides — the benchmark does not prescribe it.
unknown_handling: The benchmark measures how agents handle unknowns through its task design. Tasks are sourced from real release notes with ambiguous, high-level descriptions. Agents that fail to clarify ambiguous requirements, make incorrect assumptions, or skip exploration of unknown codebase areas fail. The benchmark reveals that current agents predominantly handle unknowns poorly: 60%+ failures are requirement understanding errors (agent did not fully understand what the task required), and providing additional PR/Issue context improved performance by less than 5 percentage points. This indicates agents cannot effectively use additional information to resolve unknowns — they have already reached an information-integration ceiling, not an information-access one.
default_stance_on_unknown: SWE-EVO implicitly favors a RESTRICTIVE stance — making changes in a mature codebase with 874 tests means that any incorrect assumption about an unknown aspect of the codebase likely triggers a test failure. However, the agents being evaluated are typically PERMISSIVE (they make changes and hope tests pass), which accounts for the low success rates. The benchmark does not prescribe a stance — it measures the consequences of the agent's chosen stance.
safety_guardrails: SWE-EVO is a benchmark and does not prescribe safety guardrails. For agents being evaluated, the only inherent guardrail is the test suite itself — harmful changes are caught as test failures. The PASS_TO_PASS constraint acts as a regression safety net. However, the benchmark is run on isolated task instances, not production systems, so there are no operational safety concerns. The benchmark's seven source projects (scikit-learn, pydantic, dvc, dask, requests, modin, conan) are well-established open-source projects with mature test suites, which serve as de facto safety validation.
human_decision_points: None within the benchmark itself — evaluation is fully automated. For the agents being evaluated, the human decision point is at the task assignment level (which tasks to delegate to the agent), not during execution. The benchmark measures fully autonomous agent performance. The gap between SWE-bench (65-73%) and SWE-EVO (21-25%) strongly suggests that human-in-the-loop intervention would be beneficial at the decomposition and requirement-clarification stages — precisely where agents are failing.
override_capability: Not applicable to the benchmark itself. For evaluated agents, override capability depends on the agent framework used (OpenHands, SWE-Agent, etc.). The benchmark does not constrain or measure override mechanisms.
fallback_when_no_human: The benchmark evaluates fully autonomous execution — agents must complete tasks without human intervention. When agents encounter situations they cannot handle, the observed failure modes are: producing incorrect code that fails tests, generating syntactically invalid output, or entering repetitive loops (especially GPT-5-nano and smaller models). There is no built-in fallback — the agent either completes the task or fails.
max_problem_size: Demonstrated: 48 tasks from 7 mature Python projects, averaging 21 files modified, 610 lines changed, 874 tests per instance, 2,390-word requirement descriptions. Theoretical limit: not bounded by the benchmark design — tasks could be larger, but the practical limit is the test suite size (the evaluation must be automatable). What breaks at scale: agent performance drops to near zero as task complexity increases. The 21% resolution rate for GPT-5 on tasks of this scale suggests this is already near the frontier of what current LLM agents can handle. The benchmark reveals that file count is not the bottleneck — requirement comprehension is.
parallel_execution: SWE-EVO does not prescribe parallel or sequential execution — agents can use either. The task structure (multi-file changes with dependencies) naturally supports parallelism for independent file modifications but requires sequential coordination for dependent changes. Whether agents exploit this parallelism is an implementation choice. The benchmark results do not break down performance by parallelism strategy. The tasks' dependency structures are unknown to the agent a priori, so discovering parallelization opportunities is itself part of the challenge.
team_scaling_model: Not directly addressed by SWE-EVO — all evaluated agents are single-agent systems (OpenHands, SWE-Agent). However, the benchmark design strongly suggests that multi-agent approaches would be beneficial: different agents could handle different files or different phases (understand, implement, test). The paper notes that SWE-EVO is classified under Multiagent Systems (cs.MA) as a secondary subject, indicating the authors see multi-agent approaches as relevant. The observed bottleneck (requirement understanding at 60%+ of failures) suggests that adding more implementation agents would not help — the bottleneck is cognitive, not throughput-limited.
cost_scaling_model: Not evaluated by the benchmark. Inference cost depends on the agent framework and model used. GPT-5-level models are needed to achieve 21-25% resolution, and cost scales with the large context requirements (2,390-word descriptions + multi-file codebase context). The benchmark reveals that cost increases sub-linearly per additional file (changing 21 files is not 10x harder than changing 2 files — it is 2-3x harder, suggesting logarithmic cost scaling with file count but linear with requirement complexity). The Fix Rate metric captures partial progress that would be wasted as sunk cost in binary evaluation.
replanning_built_in: Not prescribed by the benchmark. Agents can replan if their chosen approach fails tests, but the benchmark does not mandate or measure replanning specifically. The Fix Rate metric can indirectly capture replanning effectiveness: high Fix Rate with low Pass Rate may indicate replanning that makes partial progress but cannot converge. The benchmark's difficulty suggests replanning is essential — agents that stick to a single plan fail at very high rates.
trigger_mechanism: Not prescribed. In practice, the test suite serves as the trigger: failing tests indicate the current approach is wrong. Agents can use test failures as adaptation triggers. The benchmark does not enforce a specific trigger mechanism.
adaptation_cost: Not measured. The Fix Rate metric captures the gap between adaptation cost and success — agents that partially adapt get partial credit. Full replanning cost would be measured in tokens and wall-clock time, but the benchmark does not report these per-task metrics.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Not prescribed by the benchmark. The Fix Rate metric provides a framework for measuring partial recovery: an agent that fixes 27% of a task's requirements but fails the full validation gets Fix Rate 27% rather than Pass Rate 0%. Actual recovery mechanisms (retries, alternative approaches, test-driven iteration) depend on the agent framework. The benchmark reveals that current agents have poor recovery: once they misunderstand a requirement, they rarely self-correct. The <5% improvement from additional context confirms this — agents cannot effectively use new information to recover from initial misunderstandings.
output_artifacts: The benchmark produces: (1) Pass Rate score per model per task; (2) Fix Rate score capturing partial progress; (3) per-task pass/fail status with corresponding test outputs; (4) aggregated statistics across all 48 tasks; (5) the generated code changes (diffs) for qualitative analysis. The benchmark also categorizes failures by type (requirement understanding, tool use errors, syntax errors, logic errors, repetitive loops).
briefing_format: SWE-EVO tasks are presented as high-level requirement descriptions (average 2,390 words) in natural language, simulating how a product manager or release note would describe a feature. This is unstructured natural language, not a formal specification — the agent must interpret it into actionable changes. This briefing format deliberately tests whether agents can handle the ambiguity of real-world requirements.
traceability: Strong within the benchmark: every task instance is linked to a specific project release note, with a ground-truth patch and test suite. This enables tracing: requirement → expected changes → test validation. For agents, the benchmark can trace which decisions led to which test failures (through the Fix Rate metric's per-test attribution). The paper categorizes failures by root cause type, providing traceability from outcome to error class.
agent_communication_protocol: SWE-EVO does not prescribe an inter-agent protocol — all evaluated configurations use single-agent frameworks. However, the benchmark's task structure (21 files with dependencies across them) maps naturally to multi-agent scenarios where agents communicate about file ownership, dependency resolution, and integration testing. The paper's classification under cs.MA indicates this is an intended future direction. The benchmark's output format (diffs, test results) provides a natural shared artifact format for multi-agent coordination.
learning_memory_architecture: Not applicable to SWE-EVO itself — it is a static benchmark, not a learning system. The benchmark can be used to measure whether agents improve over time (e.g., through fine-tuning on SWE-EVO-like tasks), but this is not built into the benchmark design. Future use cases include: using SWE-EVO as a training signal for reinforcement learning, as an evaluation set for fine-tuned coding models, and as a regression test suite for agent framework improvements.
latency_bounds: Not prescribed by the benchmark. Tasks are evaluated offline with no real-time requirements. The practical latency for a single task depends on the agent framework and model — GPT-5 agents typically take minutes to tens of minutes per task. The benchmark does not impose time limits. Under time pressure, agents would likely perform even worse, as the primary bottleneck (requirement understanding) benefits from deliberation.
evaluation_benchmarking: SWE-EVO is a benchmark designed to evaluate coding agents, not a method being evaluated. It is validated against: (1) SWE-Bench Verified — showing the capability gap between single-issue and evolutionary tasks; (2) multiple frontier models — GPT-5, GPT-5-nano, GPT-5.4, Kimi-K2, and various open-source models; (3) multiple agent frameworks — OpenHands and SWE-Agent. Key metrics: Pass Rate (binary task success), Fix Rate (partial progress). The benchmark itself is validated through its construction methodology (real release notes, mature projects, large test suites) and its ability to discriminate between model capabilities where SWE-Bench cannot (GPT-5.4: 25% on SWE-EVO vs 72.8% on SWE-Bench Verified).
mapping_strength: Very strong mapping to JORDAN and agentic software development. SWE-EVO directly validates JORDAN's core thesis: that plan-decompose-execute workflows are necessary because single-pass solutions fail catastrophically on multi-file tasks. The benchmark's findings support JORDAN's pipeline design: (1) requirement understanding (60%+ of failures) maps to JORDAN's plan and research nodes — agents need explicit planning and research phases, not just code generation; (2) multi-file coordination maps to JORDAN's execute node with DAG-based task decomposition; (3) test-driven validation maps to JORDAN's synthesize node; (4) the 21-25% ceiling maps to JORDAN's need for replanning — agents must recover from failed approaches. SWE-EVO provides the quantitative evidence that JORDAN's multi-phase architecture is targeting the right bottleneck.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN integration: (1) SWE-EVO measures agent performance but does not prescribe how to improve it — it identifies the bottleneck (decomposition) without providing a solution; (2) the benchmark is Python-only (7 projects, all Python), so its findings may not generalize to other languages or project types; (3) SWE-EVO evaluates single-agent systems; it does not measure multi-agent coordination benefits, which is central to JORDAN's architecture; (4) the benchmark does not measure intermediate planning quality — only final outcomes — so it cannot distinguish between 'good plan, bad execution' and 'bad plan'; (5) Fix Rate is a proxy for partial progress but does not capture which specific sub-tasks were completed correctly; (6) the 48-task dataset is small compared to SWE-Bench's scale, limiting statistical power for model comparison; (7) the benchmark evaluates coding tasks, not research tasks or multi-modal tasks, so it only covers part of JORDAN's scope.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) SWE-EVO should be adopted as a primary evaluation benchmark for JORDAN's execute node and plan node — measuring whether JORDAN's decomposition and execution improve over single-agent baselines on multi-file tasks; (2) JORDAN should be evaluated on SWE-EVO with its full pipeline (plan → research → risk_assessment → execute → synthesize → replan) vs. a single-agent baseline, testing whether the pipeline overhead is justified by success rate improvements; (3) JORDAN's replan node should be tested specifically on SWE-EVO tasks where initial attempts fail — measuring whether replanning recovers from the requirement-understanding errors that dominate current failures; (4) the risk_assessment node could be validated by measuring whether it correctly identifies high-risk tasks in SWE-EVO (tasks touching many files, critical infrastructure); (5) JORDAN's research node could be tested on SWE-EVO tasks to see whether codebase exploration improves requirement understanding, addressing the 60%+ failure category.
validation_context: Validated on 48 tasks from 7 mature open-source Python projects (scikit-learn, pydantic, dvc, dask, requests, modin, conan). Tasks constructed from real release notes with ground-truth patches. Evaluated against multiple frontier models (GPT-5, GPT-5-nano, GPT-5.2, GPT-5.4, Kimi-K2) using OpenHands and SWE-Agent frameworks. Peer-reviewed through arXiv (submitted December 2025, updated through v5 in April 2026). The benchmark is publicly available on GitHub (github.com/SWE-EVO/SWE-EVO).
known_failures: The benchmark reveals these failure modes in evaluated agents: (1) Requirement misunderstanding — 60%+ of GPT-5 failures, the dominant failure mode; agents implement the wrong thing or miss key requirements from the 2,390-word descriptions; (2) Implementation logic errors — ~70% of failures for open-source models like Kimi-K2; agents write code that superficially addresses the requirement but contains logical flaws; (3) Tool use errors, syntax errors, and repetitive loops — primarily affecting smaller models (GPT-5-nano class); (4) Integration failures — agents make correct changes in isolation but fail to coordinate them, causing PASS_TO_PASS violations; (5) Information integration failure — providing additional PR/Issue context improves performance by <5 percentage points, indicating agents cannot effectively use auxiliary information. For the benchmark itself: the 48-task dataset may be too small for high-confidence model ranking, and the Python-only scope limits generalizability.
where_still_used: Active as a research benchmark in the AI coding agent community. The GitHub repository (github.com/SWE-EVO/SWE-EVO) provides the benchmark infrastructure. The paper is under active revision (v5 as of April 2026) with increasing detail. Likely to be adopted by major AI labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind) for frontier model evaluation, given that SWE-Bench has become the standard and SWE-EVO exposes the next capability frontier. Expected to be integrated into agent evaluation suites (SWE-bench ecosystem, OpenHands, SWE-Agent) during 2026.
